title: About
text: As a rapid geospatial assessment, the City Scan uses the best publicly available global datasets and open-source tools to package together maps, data, visualizations, and insights about the built and natural environments. With a City Scan in hand, Readers can begin to think more spatially about the critical resilience challenges that their areas of interest may face. The City Scan can serve as a structured body of strong evidence that can help raise good questions and fuel critical conversations to support deeper understanding around pressing urban resilience challenges, if it is read with both possibilities and limitations in mind.

title: Population Growth
text:
What Is It?
Population figures are typically used as a denominator for many indicators, and are a measure of demand for services. The line chart above summarizes the city’s population change according to census and other public data. High growth of urban populations, caused by rates of natural increase (more births than deaths) in urban areas, migration from rural to urban areas, and the transformation of rural settlements into urban places, puts pressure on cities to meet the new demand

title: Population Growth
text:
Why Use It?
Population growth is a key driver metric for understanding the pace and nature of urban change. They help identify whether growth is steady, accelerating, or declining—insights that are critical for planning housing, transportation, utilities, schools, healthcare, among other essential services and infrastructure. Tracking population growth also allows comparison with other urban indicators, such as job creation, revealing whether infrastructure and service provision is keeping pace with demand.

title: Population Growth
text:
How Is It Made?
Population growth data are typically compiled from national or local censuses, statistical agencies, and international databases. Intermediate years between census rounds may be estimated using demographic models that incorporate birth and death rates, migration statistics, and household survey data.

title: Population Growth
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
rising population trend may indicate migration inflows, high natural birth rates, or a growing economy, while a declining trend could point to out-migration, aging demographics, or economic stagnation. Sudden increases may reflect administrative reclassification rather than actual migration or birth spikes. Conversely, a drastic decline can be a result of natural disasters, pandemics, or any other catastrophe. Population growth rates can be relevant for comparisons with changes in land area, density (especially when it is driving a rapid urban sprawl), and infrastructure provision.

title: Population Distribution by Age and Sex
text:
What Is It?
This column chart classifies the city’s population according to age group, forming the basis of population projections. Populations vary significantly in their proportions of young and old people, with younger age structures typically associated with growing populations, and older ones with slower or negative growth. Reproductive age is defined as 15–49. Working age is defined as 15–64.
crumbs: Population Distribution by Age and Sex

title: Population Distribution by Age and Sex
text:
Why Use It?
Age–sex distribution provides critical insight into labor supply, dependency ratios, and future service demand. A larger working-age population can boost economic productivity, while high proportions of children or older adults signal greater needs for education, health care, and social protection.
crumbs: Population Distribution by Age and Sex

title: Population Distribution by Age and Sex
text:
How Is It Made?
Population data by age and sex are derived from censuses and projections, then disaggregated using gridded population models. For this dataset, counts are grouped into standard five-year intervals, separated by sex, and aggregated to the city level. This provides a consistent, comparable view of the age–sex structure across different urban contexts.
crumbs: Population Distribution by Age and Sex

title: Population Distribution by Age and Sex
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
The shape of the age–sex distribution can offer important clues about a city’s demographic dynamics and future trajectory. A population dominated by younger cohorts may suggest demographic momentum and potential for future growth. A broad working-age population (15–64) may reflect the capacity for greater productivity. In contrast, a higher share of older residents may point to emerging needs in healthcare, care systems, and inclusive urban design.

title: Warm Spell Duration Index (WSDI)
text:
What Is It?
Warm Spell Duration Index (WSDI) is the total number of days in a year that are part of a heatwave of 6 days or longer, when the daily maximum temperature is higher than in 90% of days in the reference period 1995-2014.

title: Warm Spell Duration Index (WSDI)
text:
Why Use It?
WSDI is an important measure of heatwaves, an increasingly common phenomenon in Bangladesh and around the world. Heatwaves not only pose a direct threat to urban areas by harming residents and industries, especially vulnerable populations, but also jeopardize people’s basic needs by undermining agricultural productivity and water supply in more rural areas, resulting in ripple effects like accelerating migration to urban areas and intensifying resource shortage.

title: Climate Projections
section: What Is This, and How Was It Made?
text:
What Is This, and How Was It Made?
Climate projection models are tools that scientists use to predict how the Earth’s climate might change in the future. These models simulate the interactions between the atmosphere, oceans, land, and ice, taking into account factors like greenhouse gas emissions and natural climate processes. By running these models under different scenarios, scientists can estimate potential changes in temperature, precipitation, sea level, and other climate-related factors over time. This helps us understand and prepare for future climate conditions.
Because the climate is very complex, different models might emphasize various aspects of the climate system or use slightly different assumptions, so by combining them into a multi-model ensemble, scientists can average out individual biases and gain a more robust and reliable prediction of future climate changes. This approach also helps identify where models agree or disagree, improving confidence in the results.
The climate projections in this analysis are directly adapted from the Climate Change Knowledge Portal (CCKP), which computes a range of climate indicators using median values of multi-model ensembles derived from the sixth phase of the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP6). Up to 31 CMIP6 models are used in the CCKP-CMIP6 collection. CCKP defines the 20-year window of 1995-2014 as the reference period, and any future projections are compared against the climate condition during this reference period, so that we can gauge the magnitude of climatic changes. The future period used in this analysis is 2024-2100.
Moreover, SSP scenarios, or Shared Socioeconomic Pathways, are a set of narratives that describe different possible futures based on varying levels of social, economic, and environmental development. These scenarios are used in climate modeling to explore how different societal choices might impact greenhouse gas emissions and, consequently, climate change.
To disentangle the deep uncertainties embedded in climate models and to effectively inform decision-making on adaptation strategies, this analysis presents climate projections for X Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs): [TBD], each with its corresponding levels of designated Radiative Forcing (W/m2) by 2100 (radiative forcing is a measure of how much the Earth’s energy balance is changed by factors like greenhouse gases, aerosols, or changes in solar energy).

title: Climate Projections
text:
Why Use It?
Bangladesh is particularly vulnerable to climate change due to its low-lying geography, dense population, and reliance on agriculture. The country faces increased risks of severe flooding, stronger cyclones, rising sea levels, and more intense heatwaves. These changes threaten homes, crops, and livelihoods, especially in coastal and rural areas. Climate change also exacerbates existing challenges like poverty and food security, making it crucial for Bangladesh to prepare and adapt to these growing threats.
The climate projections can help predict future risks like flooding, heatwaves, and sea-level rise. By understanding these potential changes, cities can make better decisions to protect communities, infrastructure, and livelihoods, ensuring a safer and more resilient future for the residents.

title: Climate Projections
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Climate model projections are valuable tools for understanding how climate change could impact cities, but they come with some caveats. These models are based on assumptions and scenarios that may not perfectly predict the future, so there’s some uncertainty in the results. They also operate on large scales, so local factors unique to a city might not be fully captured. To help overcome some of the limitations, we include more detailed versions of the climate projection graphs that paint a fuller picture of the full range of model predictions, so that cities have a more comprehensive understanding of the potential hazards they might confront in the upcoming decades. Whenever possible, the climate projections should be used alongside other local data and expert advice when planning for climate impacts.
This analysis focuses on indicators related to temperature and precipitation to gain a rough understanding of possible changes in future heat, flood, and cold spell hazards. The following part explains each indicator in greater detail.






NoteFootnote



CCKP Metadata.

title: Mean Temperature and Maximum of Daily Max-Temperature
text:
What Is It?
Mean temperature refers the average mean temperature over a year. The daily max-temperature refers to the maximum single day maximum temperature over a year.
crumbs: Mean Temperature and Maximum of Daily Max-Temperature

title: Mean Temperature and Maximum of Daily Max-Temperature
text:
Why Use It?
Temperature increases of up to 1.5 degrees could lead to a variety of consequences with regards to health, productivity, and natural hazards. For urban residents, higher temperatures could mean more incidents of heat-related illnesses, such as heat exhaustion, heat stroke, cardiovascular and kidney diseases, and even death. Rising temperatures could also worsen air pollution by increasing ground-level ozone smog, which is created when pollution from cars, factories, and other sources reacts to sunlight and heat. Furthermore, warmer temperatures could be linked to projected precipitation increase because the Clausius-Clapeyron-Relationship dictates that for every 1ºC of increased air temperature, that air’s potential to carry moisture increases by 7%. Thus, the warmer the air, the more moisture it “can” carry, and therefore if rain were to form, much more water could be tapped into.
Importantly, mean temperature statistics do not necessarily capture seasonal differences or extreme scenarios. The rise in summer temperatures could exceed the projected average increase, exacerbating or prolonging severe weather conditions that pose a significant threat to people’s lives and livelihoods.
crumbs: Mean Temperature and Maximum of Daily Max-Temperature

title: Days with Precipitation >20mm and >50mm
text:
What Is It?
These indicators refer to the average count of days in a year with at least 20mm and 50mm of precipitation.

title: Days with Precipitation >20mm and >50mm
text:
Why Use It?
The threshold for heavy precipitation can vary depending on local conditions and the specific impacts being assessed, but 50 millimeters is generally a good benchmark for significant rainfall events that can lead to flooding and other impacts in Bangladesh. As climate impacts are often convoluted and non-linear, it is difficult to speculate the reasons or driving forces behind the projection results. More detailed or localized assessments and modeling would be needed to understand, for example, the reason why precipitation increases in one scenario and decreases in another.

title: Flooding
text:
What Is It?
There are 12 total maps for flooding. These consist of maps for the three main types of urban flooding (fluvial, pluvial and coastal), as well as their combination, also known as compounded risk. Each of these flood types is laid over layers for population density, built-up area expansion, and urban infrastructure (including schools, health facilities, and police and fire stations) to show the areas and assets exposed to flooding.
There are three main types of urban flooding:

Fluvial or riverine flooding is floodwater from a watercourse, such as a river or canal. When the watercourse doesn’t have capacity for the high levels of water flowing through it, the water overflows into surrounding areas. This overflow may occur because the water level is higher than the banks of the watercourse, or the high flow rate might break through an embankment. Often fluvial flooding is caused by intense rainfall or snowmelt upstream, though it can also be caused by other upstream factors, such as dam releases. Because fluvial flooding originates upstream, it can happen even when an area is not experiencing extreme precipitation, or even when it’s dry.
Pluvial or rainwater flooding is floodwater that has not yet, or cannot, reach a watercourse. It is independent of an overflowing water body and it can occur in any urban area — even higher elevation areas that lie above and far from coastal and river floodplains. Pluvial flooding consists of both surface water and flash flooding, which both typically come from extreme rainfall. Surface water flooding happens when the rainwater overwhelms an area’s drainage system, (whether natural or constructed) so that it cannot absorb and move the floodwater. This flooding can occur slowly, as it may take time for the drainage system to fill. Though slow, it can be very destructive, especially as the water may sit for a long time. Flash floods, as the name indicates, are much faster. The water comes so quickly that it may flood an area even while the drainage system still has capacity – the water just hasn’t had time to reach it. These floods are often due to torrential rains in the nearby area and are aggravated by water funneling in from local places with higher elevation. Pluvial flooding of both types is worsened by more impermeable surfaces, such as concrete, which can limit water infiltration and increase the speed and amount of water running off the ground.
Coastal flooding occurs when sea water inundates or covers normally dry coastal land. Often coastal flooding happens when severe winds correspond with high tides, blowing the tidal waters landward in a storm surge. Coastal flooding can also occur, though, when tides are lower with greater winds, with tsunamis and no wind, and simply due to rising sea levels which push tides above historic levels. Due to the effects of climate change (e.g. sea level rise and an increase in extreme weather events), damage due to coastal flooding has intensified.

These maps also include the combined flood zones for these three flood types. The area of combined flooding is relevant not only for understanding the total extent of places exposed to any kind of flooding, but also because the three flood types are not always distinct: instead, they can compound, such as when a coastal storm surge raises the sea level, reducing the drainage system’s capacity to absorb surface water. Similarly, a regional precipitation event very well might cause rivers to overflow and also flood the area with surface water. The concurrence of pluvial, fluvial and coastal flooding often aggravates the damage potential they individually produce.
For more information on the population and built-up area underlays, see their individual sections.

title: Flooding
text:
Why Use It?
All three types of flooding are very pertinent to cities. First, the mere density of urban areas means that more people and more assets are exposed should a hazard occur. Second, cities frequently sit in more flood prone areas. Human settlements are very often situated near rivers for, among other things, increased water access, land fertility and mobility. While very helpful for many reasons, this location near water also increases exposure to fluvial flooding. Similarly, settlements near the sea face exposure to coastal flooding. Some common forms of built-up development have also made, and make, urban areas more flood prone. The pavement of urban areas and the use of impermeable materials, such as traditional asphalt and concrete, seal the ground and prevent rainwater from draining. In addition, urban areas often reroute or alter natural water ways and drainage routes.

Flooding can have devastating effect on a place and its people, through injury, destruction and disruption. It can cause
immediate harm to people, as the floodwater’s depth and velocity can cause death and injury;
damage to buildings and other assets, such as homes, schools, bridges, markets, agricultural crops, businesses, industrial facilities and cultural sites;
disruption of social and economic life, as it restricts movement and access and causes power outages;
environmental damage, as the flooding causes and carries leaks of pollutants; and
displacement, as people leave their homes to avoid all of these harms.

By knowing where flooding is most likely to occur (as well as its likely particular causes and its origins), cities are more able to decrease both exposure and vulnerability. This knowledge can guide urban development and the siting of new projects away from exposed areas, and inform where flood mitigation projects should be focused.
A variety of responses are possible, including both structural and non-structural interventions. Structural interventions include “blue” infrastructure, like retention ponds, bioswales, water squares, underground storage and floodplain extension; “green” infrastructure, like mangroves, salt marshes and green roofs; and “grey” infrastructure, like dikes, canals, pump stations, storm surge barriers, flood walls and sea walls. Non-structural interventions include warning systems, emergency institutions, and changes to building codes. A spatial understanding of a city’s flood exposure can direct a city’s efforts on where to build flood infrastructure, where not to build non-flood projects, and how to spatially adapt non-structural interventions.
The overlay with population density indicates which areas of expected flooding would likely affect the most people. It also indicates the kinds of preparedness and coordination different areas may require during a flood disaster. Areas with greater population density may likely require greater resource allocation and longer evacuation times.
Within a city’s boundaries, not all land is developed. The overlay with built-up area shows how the flood zone intersects with where buildings are. This juxtaposition is helpful for multiple reasons. First, it indicates different types of exposure. Flooding in built-up areas and non-built-up areas can both be problematic, but the kind of impact will differ as the former would damage buildings while the second may destroy crops and pollute water sources. Second, as stated previously, urban areas often comprise impervious surfaces, a map of built-up area shows how pluvial flooding may be increased in developed zones. Third, the map of built-up area shows the physical expansion of the city in recent decades. It may indicate how urban factors such as land price, zoning, etc., are guiding the city toward or away from flood zones. Knowing whether newer or older construction is more common in flood zones can also indicate the likely types of construction in the flood zones, and how flood resilient they likely are.
The social infrastructure underlay maps where hospitals, schools, fire stations and police stations are located, and is especially helpful for understanding a city’s disaster response. These services are obviously important to a city’s functioning and so it is important to know their exposure. Even more so, however, each of these services and facilities are central in the immediate aftermath of a disaster. Flooding can cause serious injury, and so access to hospitals and health clinics is imperative. If these facilities are themselves exposed to flooding, they are less able to meet the needs of flooding’s victims elsewhere in the city. Similarly, fire and police services are often first responders in the wake of a crisis: if they are exposed to flooding, they are less able to respond. Finally, while the educational aspect of schools may be less pertinent during and immediately after a flood event, schools are often larger public buildings which can house displaced peoples.

title: Flooding
text:
How Is It Made?
The global flood map begins with a high resolution global map of elevation: water flows downhill and fills basins, so it’s important to know where the downhills and basins are. This elevation map uses the Copernicus Digital Elevation Model, but then removes surface objects like buildings and forests. This terrain model, though, does not include river depths – it shows rivers’ water surfaces not the river beds. Water channel depth, then, needs to be derived by estimating water flow through the channel and calculating the necessary depth of each cross section for that flow. Onto this adapted terrain model, the flood model then layers on hydrodynamic models simulating how water moves across terrain during extreme river flow and rainfall events, and hydrological models simulating how water moves across water systems. Finally, the frequency and magnitude of extreme precipitation events (as well as how they are expected to change with climate change) are factored in. The resulting model has a critical success index (ratio of total observed events and predicted events that were both true events and predicted) of 0.75.
To read more about Fathom’s methodology, read about their “method stack” here. Each step in the process includes research articles which go further in depth.

title: Flooding
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
These maps show the 15-cm flood zones with 1-in-10, 1-in-100 and 1-in-1000 year return periods. That is, they show the areas that are expected to flood to a depth of at least 15 centimeters at least once in a 10-year, 100-year or 1,000 year span. It is important to note, though, that due to the effects of climate change, with its change in precipitation levels and increase in extreme events, the expected frequency of flooding is changing. In general, flooding is expected to become more frequent and more severe.

title: Seismic Risk
text:
Methodology
This map indicates the geographic distribution of average annual loss (AAL) of built-up area due to ground shaking in the residential, commercial, and industrial building stock, considering contents, structural, and non-structural components. It does not consider the effects of tsunamis, liquefaction, landslides, and fires following earthquakes. The loss estimates are from direct physical damage to buildings due to shaking, and thus damage to infrastructure or indirect losses due to business interruption are not included. Seismic risk in cities has increased mainly due to urbanization, poor land-use planning and construction, inadequate infrastructure and services, and environmental degradation. Urban risk predictions and expected losses from major earthquakes in the future justify proactive risk mitigation activities.
Map data from Silva et al, 2023, Global Earthquake Model (GEM) Seismic Hazard Map and Seismic Risk Map, V2023.1.
These data and map are derived from the Global Earthquake Model, which provides information on probabilistic earthquake risk; at its scale, it does not address the vulnerability to earthquakes of individual structures or sub-regions. An earthquake with moderate damage potential can cause structural damage to unreinforced masonry buildings and the movement of wood-frame houses. See here for more.

title: Coastal Erosion
text:
What Is It?
Coastal erosion refers to the long-term landward retreat of the shoreline caused by natural marine and atmospheric processes such as wave action, currents, storm surges, and sea-level rise. This process has been accelerated by unregulated human activities (such as coastal urbanization and unsustainable tourism) that alter sediment transport.
Coastal erosion leads to shoreline retreat and beach lowering, threatens home, roads and other critical infrastructure, and causes a particular disruption of activities such as agriculture, fisheries, and tourism, increasing the risk of flooding.
Accretion occurs when there is an accumulation of sediment such as sand, silt, or organic matter, and the coast is advancing seawards. While this may seem positive, it often indicates sand being trapped by man-made structures such as groynes or jetties, which can starve downdrift shorelines of sediment and lead to increased coastal erosion in those areas .

title: Coastal Erosion
text:
Why Use It?
Coastlines are dynamic and continuously shaped by waves, currents, sediment transport, sea-level rise, and human interventions. This map helps identify spatial patterns of shoreline retreat and advance, allowing cities to assess where existing buildings, roads, and critical infrastructure may face increasing exposure to coastal hazards.
By localizing these areas, municipalities can implement programs of protection (through hard-protection interventions, sediment-based solutions, or ecosystem-based adaptation), accommodation, advance (through creating and raising land), or retreat.
Areas experiencing accretion indicate shoreline progradation, which may create opportunities for land-use planning, ecosystem restoration, or protective buffer expansion. However, accretion can also reflect altered sediment dynamics and may not represent long-term stability.

title: Coastal Erosion
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
The length and color of the transects extending perpendicular to the shoreline represent the rate of shoreline change (measured as meters per year) and its direction. Negative values (warm colors) indicate landward retreat (erosion), while positive values (cool colors) indicate seaward advance (accretion). Yellow denotes near-zero change, suggesting relative shoreline stability.
Dark red segments represent high erosion rates (≥ 3 m/year of retreat), indicating high-hazard zones where infrastructure and assets may face immediate exposure. Orange reflects moderate erosion (approximately −1.5 m/year). Light and dark green segments represent accretion rates of approximately +1.5 m/year and ≥ +3 m/year, respectively, indicating shoreline progradation.

title: Lightning Flash Rate Density
text:
What Is It?
The lightning flash rate density here refers to the mean annual flash rate density, which is the total lightning flashes per year per square kilometer. It comes from a NASA dataset called the LIS 0.1 Degree Very High Resolution Gridded Lightning Full Climatology (VHRFC) dataset, which measures the distribution and variability of total lightning occurring in the Earth’s tropical and subtropical regions.






NoteFootnote



“LIS 0.1 DEGREE VERY HIGH RESOLUTION GRIDDED LIGHTNING FULL CLIMATOLOGY (VHRFC) V1.” n.d. NASA. https://cmr.earthdata.nasa.gov/search/concepts/C1979883245-GHRC_DAAC.html.

title: Lightning Flash Rate Density
text:
Why Use It?

This information can be used for severe storm detection and analysis, which can help cities prepare for and prevent potential lightning damages.
The Khasi Hills, on the border between India and Bangladesh, is one of the lightning hotspots in Asia.
United Nations (UN)data shows that Bangladesh loses 300 lives per year due to lightning strikes, a figure that is starkly higher compared to that of the United States, with less than 20 deaths per year.
Deaths caused by lightning are rising in Bangladesh, and extreme weather caused by climate change is to blame for the increase in fatalities.







NoteFootnote



Albrecht, Rachel I., Steven J. Goodman, Dennis E. Buechler, Richard J. Blakeslee, and Hugh J. Christian. 2016. “Where Are the Lightning Hotspots on Earth?,” November. https://doi.org/10.1175/BAMS-D-14-00193.1.
“Bangladesh: Climate Change Is Increasing Lightning Deaths.” 2024. PreventionWeb. January 26, 2024. https://www.preventionweb.net/news/climate-change-increasing-lightning-deaths-bangladesh.

title: Lightning Flash Rate Density
text:
How Is It Made?

The lightning flash measurements are taken by the Lightning Imaging Sensor (LIS) instrument on board the NASA Tropical Rainfall Measuring Mission (TRMM) launched in November 1997. It is designed to detect lightning from space during both day and night with storm-scale resolution. The LIS was powered off and the TRMM mission ended data collection on 8 April 2015, terminating a mission lasting a total of 17+ years.
TRMM started its descending path to decommissioning in 2014 with several instrument outages during the period, which the dataset authors expect could introduce uncertainties on the lightning data. Therefore, 2014 and 2015 data were not included in the dataset.







NoteFootnote



Albrecht, Rachel I., Steven J. Goodman, Dennis E. Buechler, Richard J. Blakeslee, and Hugh J. Christian. 2016. “Where Are the Lightning Hotspots on Earth?,” November. https://doi.org/10.1175/BAMS-D-14-00193.1.

title: Lightning Flash Rate Density
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
The number of lightning flashes is only one factor in the fatalities caused by lightnings. The majority of victims of lightning are farmers, who are vulnerable to the elements as they work in the fields through the rainy monsoon months in the spring and summer. Therefore, higher lightning flash rate density does not necessarily mean higher fatality rates. The vulnerability of the local population—especially outdoor workers during monsoons—plays a big part as well.






NoteFootnote



Vaidyanathan, Rajini. 2023. “Bangladesh Sees Dramatic Rise in Lightning Deaths Linked to Climate Change.” BBC, December 31, 2023. https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-67779223.

title: Seismic Hazard
text:
Methodology
This map depicts the geographic distribution of the Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) with a 10% probability of being exceeded in 50 years, computed for reference rock conditions (shear wave velocity of 760-800 m/s). Seismic risk in cities has increased mainly due to urbanization, poor land-use planning and construction, inadequate infrastructure and services, and environmental degradation. Urban risk predictions and expected losses from major earthquakes in the future justify proactive risk mitigation activities.
These data and map are derived from the Global Earthquake Model, which provides information on probabilistic earthquake risk; at its scale, it does not address the vulnerability to earthquakes of individual structures or sub-regions. An earthquake with moderate damage potential can cause structural damage to unreinforced masonry buildings and the movement of wood-frame houses. See here for more.

title: Elevation
text:
What Is It?
Elevation is a measure of how many meters above sea level an area is. Elevation informs an area’s vulnerability to many natural disasters.

title: Elevation
text:
Why Use It?
The height at which infrastructure, resources, and communities sit relative to normal water levels and tides, flood waters, and storm surges and waves informs their exposure. Elevation information is critical for communities to anticipate the impacts of disasters and to prepare resilient and cost-effective response and redevelopment strategies.






TipExample



In Bangladesh:
Much of Bangladesh is a low-lying delta with elevations ranging from just a few meters above sea level to below sea level in some areas. This makes it extremely vulnerable to flooding, especially during monsoon season.
The country is prone to severe cyclones and storm surges, exacerbated by its low elevation. These events can cause disruption to services and destroy critical infrastructure.
Many urban communities lie on the coast, which can be only a few meters above sea level or even below sea level, exposing highly dense populations to major climactic threats.
Riverbank erosion and sediment deposition are common problems in Bangladesh. The elevation of land can change rapidly due to sediment buildup or erosion, which can undermine infrastructure and lead to the loss of valuable land.

title: Elevation
text:
How Is It Made?
Elevation data comes from Fathom’s FABDEM module as well as local terrain data, creating a blended digital terrain model. The combination of FABDEM data with available LiDAR data, Fathom has elevation coverage of the entire globe at a 30-meter resolution (with finer resolution in areas with more enhanced existing data). The data is provided as meters above sea level, which is then further categorized into various increments of 160-180 meters.

title: Elevation
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Elevation data has critical implications for resilience planning, disaster management and resource allocation. Low-lying areas closer to sea level, especially coastal areas, should be further analyzed to see the resilience of the infrastructure, withstanding disaster intervention programs, and other adaptive strategies to circumvent risks associated with flooding.

title: Drought
text:
What Is It?
Drought is a prolonged and exceptional period of insufficient water availability affecting ecosystems and communities, typically resulting from below-average rainfall, high temperatures and/or strong winds. These events are called megadroughts when they are very lengthy (usually a decade or more) and pervasive.
Drought can be classified based on its origin and the part of the water system affected:

Meteorological: a period characterized by an abnormal precipitation deficit.
Agricultural and ecological: a period of abnormal soil moisture deficit resulting from reduced precipitation and/or increased evapotranspiration, affecting crop production or the regular function of ecosystems.
Hydrological: a period of sustained deficits in surface water (rivers, lakes, reservoirs) and groundwater levels.

title: Drought
text:
Why Use It?
Considered one of the costliest natural hazards, droughts can negatively impact agriculture and food security, hydropower generation and industrial activity, human and animal health, livelihood security, personal safety (for example, when women must travel long distances to collect water) and access to education, healthcare or other essential services. These impacts may also lead to conflict and population displacement, increasing strain on infrastructure and services in receiving settlements. In addition, persistent vegetation and soil moisture stress can contribute to land degradation, reduced crop yields, less capacity of CO₂ sequestration, loss of biodiversity, higher surface temperatures, increased wildfire risk, and desertification in arid and semi-arid areas.

title: Drought
text:
How Is It Made?
The Normalized Difference Moisture Index (NDMI) is derived from satellite surface reflectance data and is designed to estimate vegetation water content. It is calculated using reflectance values from the Near Infrared (NIR) and Shortwave Infrared (SWIR) spectral bands as follows:
NDMI=(NIR - SWIR) / (NIR + SWIR)

title: Drought
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
The Normalized Difference Moisture Index (NDMI) detects moisture content in vegetation, and is an indicator of water stress in crops. The stresses introduced by low soil moisture are borne by agricultural crops and biodiversity. It is also used to identify vegetation in dry areas with an increased risk of combustion. NDMI measures soil moisture from a range of -1 to 1. Negative values indicate water stress, and positive values may indicate water-logging.

title: Economic Hotspots
text:
What Is It?
Nighttime light radiance – measured as the monthly average brightness observed in satellite imagery – is used here as a proxy for economic activity. Areas with higher levels of artificial light typically correspond to higher urbanization and more commercial and industrial activity.

title: Economic Hotspots
text:
Why Use It?
Nighttime lights data provides a continuous global monitoring of economic activity, particularly useful where economic activity data is scarce or outdated. As the data is collected consistently at the same time each night, it is possible to make temporal comparisons. While most global economic indicator data is available at a national level, nighttime lights can be viewed and analyzed and smaller spatial resolutions, which is significantly beneficial for an analysis at the city or neighborhood level. It also offers a unique advantage in capturing informal economic activity, which is often absent from conventional datasets. Nighttime light radiance is also used, at times, as a proxy for wealth – and indeed it is a feature in the relative wealth model.

title: Economic Hotspots
text:
How Is It Made?
Nighttime light data is collected by satellites equipped with Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS), which cross the equator at around 01:30 a.m. local time. The data is collected, geolocated, and calibrated, thus creating a daily value of nanowatts per square centimeter per steradian— a measure of radiance indicating how much light is emitted from a surface area in a given direction. The daily measurements are aggregated into an average monthly value. This map shows the cumulative monthly radiance for the past 10 years.

title: Economic Hotspots
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Higher nighttime light emissions generally indicate higher levels of economic activity. However, it is important to emphasize that nighttime lights are a proxy, not a direct measure of economic output. They offer valuable insight into the spatial patterns of economic activity, infrastructure development, and urbanization – but must be interpreted in context. While increased brightness often corresponds to urban growth or infrastructure expansion, it can also reflect the spread of informal settlements, which may not align with improved living conditions or broader economic development outcomes. Conversely, areas with limited electrification may appear dim despite active local economies, leading to potential underestimation of economic activity. Different types of economic activity generate varying levels of nighttime light. For example, a gas station may emit more light than a daytime market, even if the latter plays a more central role in the local economy. Nighttime light data is best used to complement – not replace – other socioeconomic data, offering a spatially detailed lens on patterns of activity that are often missed in conventional indicators.

title: Economic Hotspots
text:
Source Data Citation

Mills, Stephen, Stephanie Weiss, and Calvin Liang. 2013. “VIIRS Day/Night Band (DNB) Stray Light Characterization and Correction.” In Earth Observing Systems XVIII, 8866:549–66. SPIE. https://doi.org/10.1117/12.2023107.

title: Solar
text:
What Is It?
Solar availability is the measure of solar power potential in a theoretical 1 kWp photovoltaic (PV) system. Photovoltaics (PV) is the most widely applied and versatile technology for solar power. The availability of solar energy depends on site conditions. This map displays an indicative estimate of daily specific yield, that is, how much energy a hypothetical PV system would produce for per unit of capacity (kWh/kWp). Values above 4.5 are considered excellent availability, while values between 3.5 and 4.5 are moderate.

title: Solar
text:
Why Use It?
PV offers a unique opportunity to achieve long-term energy sustainability goals. Determining parts of a city that may be optimal for PV can optimize renewable energy integration, support energy resilience, amplify economic growth and job creation, and maximize land use for energy creation efficiently.

title: Solar
text:
How Is It Made?
The data used in this map is derived from the “Global Solar Atlas 2.0,” a free online tool developed and maintained by Solargis s.r.o. for the World Bank Group. The tool leverages Solargis data and is funded by the Energy Sector Management Assistance Program (ESMAP).
Note that The Global Solar Atlas provides a preliminary assessment of photovoltaic (PV) power potential for selected locations, based on generalized theoretical conditions. The analysis assumes an optimal tilt angle for PV modules and 100% system availability, without accounting for potential downtimes.

title: Solar
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Within a city, variations in solar availability are typically minimal, with daily yields differing only slightly—for example, 4.5 kWh/kWp in one area versus 4.3 kWh/kWp in another. Because these differences are so small, PV siting decisions should consider other factors not reflected in solar availability maps, such as shading from nearby structures, roof orientation, and ease of access for maintenance. These practical considerations often outweigh the minor variations in solar yield, meaning that the map alone should not be the primary basis for choosing solar panel locations.
Additionally, solar availability varies throughout the year due to changes in cloud cover, making it crucial to assess how consistent solar energy production can be across different months. Solar systems are more sustainable when availability is relatively stable year-round, with a high-to-low availability ratio below 2:1, as this ensures that the system operates efficiently most of the time. This consistency is a key factor in determining the overall effectiveness and sustainability of solar energy installations.

title: Solar
text:

title: Forest & Deforestation
text:
What Is It?
Due to human activity and natural processes (as well as their combination), forest cover is always changing. Over the period of 2000–2012, 2.3 million square kilometers of forest were lost globally, while 0.8 million were gained, resulting in a net loss of 1.5 million square kilometers. This map shows both which areas are forested, and where there has been forest loss since 2000. Forested areas are 30 meter by 30 meter areas with at least 20% tree cover, as assessed from satellite imagery.

title: Forest & Deforestation
text:
Why Use It?
On a global scale, forests are vitally important to the mitigation of anthropogenic climate change by removing CO2 from the atmosphere. They also have important roles at the local level. Among these effects are local climate, flood and landslide prevention, biodiversity and animal habitat, water and air purification, and resource provision.
Trees draw water from deep underground and pump it to the surface. This water then evaporates from their leaves in a process called evapotranspiration. The release of water increases humidity and causes local cooling. The added moisture can then form rain clouds which both reflect sun light (increased albedo), causing further cooling, and water other plants in the surrounding area with shallower root systems. Tree roots also reduce the risk of floods and landslides, by slowing run off, strengthening soil, absorbing water, and accelerating evaporation. As it slows down the water, it also filters it, making it cleaner for drinking. Above ground, trees also act as filters, catching particulate matter on their surfaces.
To reduce global climate change and local heat, to lessen the risk of flooding and landslides, and to maintain ecological diversity, among other reasons, it is important to prevent the loss of forests. Forests are threatened by both human activity, such as deforestation for reasons of agriculture, urban expansion, and timber, and by natural processes which are often accentuated by human activity, such as wildfire and insect epidemics.

title: Forest & Deforestation
text:
How Is It Made?
For this dataset, areas of vegetation were first detected using red and infrared bands from the Landsat 7 satellite. These vegetated areas were then filtered by height, with vegetation taller than 5 meters classified as tree cover. If a 30 meter by 3- meter pixel has at least 20% tree cover, it is classified as forest. The dataset begins with a base model of forest coverage in the year 2000, and then measures forest loss for 2000–2023 and forest gain for 2000–2012.

title: Forest & Deforestation
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
As with all global remote sensing data, this dataset is not specific to local conditions or ecosystems. It may be more or less accurate with different forest types. One study has found that this global forest change dataset may overestimate deforestation in the Amazon Basin.

title: Forest & Deforestation
text:
Source Data Citation

Hansen, M. C., P. V. Potapov, R. Moore, M. Hancher, S. A. Turubanova, A. Tyukavina, D. Thau, et al. 2013. “High-Resolution Global Maps of 21st-Century Forest Cover Change.” Science 342 (6160): 850–53. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1244693.

title: Health Facilities
text:
What Is It?
Access to health facilities is measured using a network-based proximity analysis. This means calculating the shortest realistic travel distance from each location to the nearest health facility by following existing streets, rather than drawing a straight line (“as the crow flies”). Unlike simple (Euclidean) proximity analysis, which measures straight-line distance and can underestimate access in areas with fragmented street layouts, cul-de-sacs, or natural and infrastructural barriers, the network-based method accounts for actual connectivity and impedance—that is, the resistance of movement along real travel paths. In practice, this approach is similar to how navigation apps compute the shortest route to a destination. It identifies which areas are well served and which are farther from medical services. The analysis uses OpenStreetMap data, including all facilities tagged as health amenity, clinic, or hospital. Because OSM coverage is incomplete, some facilities may not be captured.

title: Health Facilities
text:
Why Use It?
Access to health facilities is a cornerstone of well-being and resilience. Proximity to hospitals and clinics is linked with better health outcomes, reduced mortality, and faster emergency response. Measuring access helps planners identify underserved neighborhoods, assess spatial equity, and prioritize investments to achieve universal health coverage goals.

title: Health Facilities
text:
How Is It Made?
Health facility locations are extracted from OpenStreetMap based on health-related tags (e.g., health amenity, clinic, hospital). Using the road network, a nearest-facility network analysis is performed to calculate the shortest distance from each point in the study area to the nearest health facility. Results are grouped into categories (e.g., <1 km, 1–2 km, 2–3 km) and mapped to visualize well-served areas and coverage gaps. Distances are computed along the mapped road network. Results do not account for slope, stairs, seasonal flooding or extreme heat, safety —especially in FCV contexts—, crossing quality, or other existing barriers unless these layers are explicitly modeled as impedances or exclusions. This approach accounts for actual travel paths rather than straight-line distances, providing a more realistic picture of accessibility.

title: Health Facilities
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Shorter network distances to health facilities are associated with greater use of preventive, curative, rehabilitative, and emergency services. As distance or travel time increases, the use of essential health services declines, since longer or more difficult trips reduce the likelihood of seeking or receiving timely care.
Areas located far from facilities—often in peripheral, rural, or low-density neighborhoods—signal potential gaps in service coverage. They may require new or upgraded health posts, improved road connectivity, or mobile and community-based care solutions. In fragile or hazard-prone contexts, physical proximity alone may not translate into effective access when safety, flooding, or impassable terrain constrain mobility.
While there is no universal threshold for “adequate” distance to health facilities, research consistently shows that the probability of using health services decreases as distance or travel time increases. The relationship is continuous rather than fixed: even small increases in distance can have meaningful effects. Hence, maps of proximity should be read as indicators of potential access—to be interpreted alongside data on facility readiness, service quality, and other contextual factors that affect people’s ability to reach care.

title: Health Facilities
text:
Source Data Citation

“OpenStreetMap.” n.d. OpenStreetMap. https://www.openstreetmap.org/.

title: Road Network Orientation (Chart Only)
text:
What Is It?
This layer visualizes the geometric orientation of a city’s streets, specifically the proportion of streets that point east, west, north, south, or to any point around a compass. Street edge bearings can help illuminate the history of urban design, physical constraints (such as topography or coastline), ancient infrastructure (such as trade routes or irrigation systems), economic geography, historical land tenure and agricultural legacy (including the shapes and boundaries of parcels), political (e.g., colonial or administrative dispositions), cultural or religious, and morphological factors, among others. It can also allow the evaluation of existing transportation patterns and configurations and the exploration of new planning proposals and alternatives.

title: Road Network Orientation (Chart Only)
text:
Why Use It?
Street edge bearings offer a valuable lens into the spatial logic and urban form of a city. By analyzing the directional orientation of streets, planners can understand the geometric structure of the street network and assess the efficiency, connectivity, and legibility of a transportation system.

title: Road Network Orientation (Chart Only)
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Cities built since the 1900s with grid-based urban planning exhibit a clear “compass rose” pattern (the majority of street bearings point in a narrow band of N-S degrees, and another narrow band of E-W degrees, e.g. Manhattan, New York City). The connectedness of a grid supports route choice, convenience, walkability, and in turn the human dynamics of social mixing, activity, and encounter. Cities with a highly organic urban form, and limited top-down planning, exhibit a heterogeneous set of edge bearings, equally spread across all corners of the compass (e.g. Kigali, Rwanda), generating a circular distribution. In other cases, partial areas within a highly planned city coexist alongside organic patterns for the remaining city, creating a hybrid pattern.

title: Built-Up Density
text:
What Is It?
This map shows the imperviousness percentage of cities’ surfaces, as measured by satellite imagery. Less pervious surfaces absorb less water. This measure is a useful proxy for the density of buildings in a built-up area: impervious surfaces are typically paved structures such as roads, parking lots, airports, etc., that are covered by water-resistant material like asphalt, concrete, or conventional rooftops.
Separate from population density, compact built-up areas can indicate where more interaction among people is likely to take place. In general, benefits of built-up density can include greater economic activity, higher energy efficiency, reduced infrastructure costs per capita, and more efficient public transport systems. While dense development can create opportunities for new public spaces or help limit urban sprawl—and, in some cases, protect peripheral green belts or natural habitats—it does not automatically guarantee increased access to open or green spaces within the built environment. Urban design and land-use planning are essential to ensure that high-density areas include adequate public spaces and to guide urban expansion in ways that prevent uncontrolled sprawl.

title: Built-Up Density
text:
Why Use It?
Built-up density is a critical spatial indicator for land-use efficiency, resilience and risk-informed planning. Areas with high concentrations of impervious surfaces are more exposed to urban heat island effects, stormwater runoff, and surface flooding—especially in the absence of adequate green or permeable infrastructure. Mapping built-up density helps identify where gray infrastructure dominates and where land is least capable of absorbing shocks. When layered with population and infrastructure data, built-up density helps identify neighborhoods where exposure to climate hazards is amplified by insufficient drainage or vulnerable constructions. It also helps prioritize where investments (such as nature-based solutions) and adaptation efforts may be most needed.
Moreover, built-up density supports more effective land-use regulation and transportation system. Higher density areas offer greater viability for mass transit systems and justify investment in transit-oriented development (TOD) by ensuring ridership and accessibility. At the same time, planning tools such as density bonuses or incentive zoning can be used in compact urban areas to secure public or green spaces in exchange for vertical development. These approaches are particularly relevant in already urbanized zones where new open spaces are scarce. Encouraging densification in targeted areas can also help limit uncontrolled sprawl, reduce travel distances, and protect ecological buffers at the urban periphery.

title: Built-Up Density
text:
Source Data Citation

title: Built-Up Area
text:
What Is It?
Built-up area refers to the spatial extent distribution of land occupied by man-made structures such as residential, commercial, and industrial buildings, roads, parks, and other pervious surfaces within the city boundaries. This map identifies when and where these changes occurred, offering a historical view of how urban sprawl has evolved over time.

title: Built-Up Area
text:
Why Use It?
Understanding the spatial footprint of urbanized areas is essential for planning the provision of public services and infrastructure. Also, understanding when the land was urbanized contextualizes the age of buildings—particularly when analyzed alongside layers on heritage sites or constructions— and infrastructure in a given area, which can have implications for the structural integrity and resilience to hazards.

title: Built-Up Area
text:
How Is It Made?
The World Settlement Footprint developed the WSF Evolution data set to map urban change between 1985 to 2015. Because existing global layers provide limited time steps and poor quality, WSF developed an iterative approach using past settlement extents using Landsat data, available from 1985 at a 30-meter resolution. Using various measures and spectral indices, WSF iteratively generated settlement and non-settlement training data to create a machine learning model to predict when settlements expanded.

title: Built-Up Area
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
The map displays when land was urbanized in 5-year ranges, which provides insight as to the spatial-temporal patterns of urban change. However, several caveats should be considered when interpreting this data. First, the WSF does not account for land that transitioned from urbanized to non-urbanized – a relatively rare but possible phenomenon. Second, any area that was already built up before 1985 is not captured in the dataset. This is particularly relevant because in some cities there may be substantial differences between settlements developed shortly before 1985 and those established decades earlier. Finally, areas can also be redeveloped, which may affect the overall accuracy of the temporal measure of urbanization.
While the year an area was urbanized can offer clues about the age of buildings and infrastructure, it’s not always a reliable proxy for structural vulnerability. For instance, many countries adopted anti-seismic building codes before 1985, and many older buildings have since been retrofitted, replaced, or demolished. For more accurate assessments of risk or building conditions, this layer should be used alongside other datasets, such as those identifying heritage sites or protected structures.

title: Built-Up Area
text:
Source Data Citation

Marconcini, Mattia, Annekatrin Metz- Marconcini, Thomas Esch, and Noel Gorelick. 2021. “Understanding Current Trends in Global Urbanisation - The World Settlement Footprint Suite.” GI_Forum 2021, Volume 9, (June): 33–38. https://doi.org/10.1553/giscience2021_01_s33.

title: Schools
text:
What Is It?
Access to schools is measured using a proximity analysis along the road network. This means calculating the shortest travel distance from each location to the nearest school following the existing streets, rather than drawing a straight line “as the crow flies”. Linear measures can underestimate access in areas with fragmented street networks, different street designs (for instance, cul-de-sacs), or existing barriers., tIn practice, this approach is similar to how navigation apps compute the shortest route to a destination. This map identifies the areas that are most- and least-accessible to schools. The analysis includes all facilities classified in OpenStreetMap as a school, kindergarten, college, or university. Note that there may be more schools that are not included in OpenStreetMap.

title: Schools
text:
Why Use It?
Measuring access to schools helps assess whether residents, especially children, can reach educational opportunities within a reasonable travel distance. Distances under about 800 meters are often considered walkable for young children, while older students may manage longer walks. Evidence indicates that greater distances to school are associated with lower enrollment, higher absenteeism and dropout, and weaker learning and health outcomes. The indicator also highlights spatial inequalities in service provision and supports planning for equity, helping to identify underserved areas, benchmark coverage, monitor progress toward universal education goals, and guide investment in new schools or mobility solutions.

title: Schools
text:
How Is It Made?
School locations are extracted from OpenStreetMap based on education tags such as school, kindergarten, college, or university. Using the road network, a network analysis is performed to calculate the shortest travel distance from each point in the study area to the nearest school. Distances are grouped into categories (e.g., <800 m, 800–1,600 m, 1,600–2,400 m), which are then mapped to show well-served areas and coverage gaps.
Distances are computed along the mapped road network. Results do not account for slope, stairs, seasonal flooding or extreme heat, safety —especially in FCV contexts—, crossing quality, or other existing barriers unless these layers are explicitly modeled as impedances or exclusions. This approach accounts for actual travel paths rather than straight-line distances, providing a more realistic picture of accessibility.

title: Schools
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Shorter network distances to schools indicate better access. Distances of under about 800 meters are generally considered walkable for young children, while distances up to 1.6 kilometers can still be manageable for older students. Beyond 2–3 kilometers, most students are unlikely to walk and are more dependent on school buses (not always available), or other transport.
Low-access areas—often in peripheral or low-density neighborhoods—signal gaps in education service coverage. They may require new schools, improved road connectivity, or targeted transport solutions. Evidence from developing contexts shows that greater distance to school can negatively affect enrollment, attendance, and even learning and health outcomes.
An important aspect to consider is that while there is no global consensus on a single “reasonable” distance to school, several benchmarks are commonly cited. Distances of around 800 m are often considered walkable for younger children, while older students may manage up to 1.6km. For cycling, feasible distances can extend to 4–8 km depending on age and conditions. In FCV or low-density settings, access is shaped as much by travel time, safety, and other context-dependent factors as by distance alone.

title: Intersections
text:
What Is It?
Intersection density measures how many street intersections exist per unit area, reflecting the compactness and potential interconnectedness of a street network. Although rough, intersection density is a powerful signal of how walkable and micromobility-friendly a place can be. The amount and types of intersections in a road network help determine how a local community functions and the character of the streets themselves. In a good street network, most streets should connect at both ends.

title: Intersections
text:
Why Use It?
Intersection density is a widely applied indicator of street network connectivity and walkability. Because it is expressed as intersections per unit area (e.g., per km2 or mi2), it allows straightforward comparison between neighborhoods, cities, or regions. The metric is also strongly linked to travel behavior: higher intersection density has been shown to correlate with more route options that support greater walking and micromobility use and lower car dependence. It also helps planners and researchers assess how permeable a street network is, identify areas with poor accessibility, and evaluate changes in urban form over time.

title: Intersections
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
A street layout with many intersections—especially grids—implies shorter blocks, more route options, fewer forced detours, and better circulation for all modes, including active and micro-mobility. That improves pedestrian comfort, disperses traffic more evenly, and supports flexible travel choices. Conversely, low intersection density—typical of superblocks, cul-de-sacs, or car-oriented layouts—means longer blocks, limited route options, and reduced permeability, which tend to discourage walking and increase car dependence.

title: Land Cover
text:
What Is It?
Unlike land use, which describes how land is used by people, the land cover layer classifies the physical surface of the Earth into distinct types including vegetation, built-up areas, water bodies, bare soil, mangroves, cropland, among others. Land cover is dynamic and can change over time due to urban expansion, deforestation, agriculture, or restoration efforts. This makes land cover a useful indicator for tracking urban growth, land degradation, or the effectiveness of conservation measures.

title: Land Cover
text:
Why Use It?
Land cover types are essential for understanding the composition and function of urban and peri-urban areas. They help planners and decision-makers evaluate how land is utilized and distributed, and assess availability of land for future development or conservation. This information is critical for zoning, land-use planning, and designing infrastructure that balances urban growth, preserveration of ecosystems, reduction of habitat loss, assessment of available land and existing risks (including heatwave, landslides and flood). It also provides a baseline to track land use changes over time—critical for monitoring urban sprawl, deforestation, and resilience to environmental shocks. This also is critical to ensure efficient and organized uses for land and maximize social and economic benefits of residential, commercial, industrial, agricultural, and conservation areas.
When combined with other layers—such as population distribution, infrastructure, or hazard maps—land cover information allows for more targeted, data-driven decision-making. For example, it can help identify areas lacking tree canopy or green space, evaluate the potential for nature-based solutions, monitor environmental degradation, or assess the balance between built and natural environments.






TipExample



In Bangladesh:
Rapid urban growth has led to an increase in urbanized land and urban sprawl into peri-urban and rural communities.
Increased demand for food has required a greater need for agricultural land, causing natural areas to be converted to agricultural fields, and the intensification of agriculture can transform land cover types
Overall increases in aquaculture too, especially in coastal areas

title: Land Cover
text:
How Is It Made?
The European Space Agency (ESA) developed the first global land cover product for the years 2020 and 2021 at a very fine resolution of 10 meters, developed and validated almost in real time based on Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data. Specialists first label certain areas of the imagery with known land cover types (such as forests, or water bodies), and then use these labeled examples to train computer models in how to classify the rest of the images. By comparing pixel characteristics such as color or texture, the model can estimate what type of land cover exists in each location. The data is organized into 11 broad land cover classes, aligned with the UN FAO’s Land Cover Classification system and the framework is aligned with the ESA’s WorldCover project.

title: Land Cover
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Mapping land cover is effective to look at the spatial distributions of land uses and also distinguish the difference between natural and man-made surfaces. Urbanized land may slightly differ from the WSF urbanized land due to the data coming from different sources. However, satellite-based imagery has limitations and there may be risks of misclassification because the accuracy is sensitive to spectral ambiguity—for example, sport fields with artificial turf may appear similar to natural grassland. For this reason, it may be necessary to complement the analysis with additional information to verify or correct anomalies.

title: Air Quality
text:
What Is It?
Air quality measures the concentration of pollutants in the air that can harm human health and the environment. In this map, it measures the ground level fine particulate matter (PM) PM2.5 in 2019, measured as micrograms per cubic meter. According to the WHO and the United States EPA standards, PM2.5 concentrations should not exceed between 5 µg/m3 or 12 µg/m3.
PM2.5 particles are those with a diameter of 2.5 micrometers or smaller which pose significant concern because their small size allows them to penetrate deep into the lungs or bloodstream. PM2.5 particles are commonly found in vehicle emissions, industrial activities, wildfires, and other sources of combustion. Other types of particulate matter, like PM10, include coarser particles like pollen, unpaved road dust, or sea salt.

title: Air Quality
text:
Why Use It?
Understanding concentration of air quality concerns in an urbanized region can influence the need for public health and environmental remediation efforts in specific neighborhoods. Monitoring air quality is crucial to promoting livability in urbanized regions.






TipExample



In Bangladesh:
Bangladesh is the world’s most polluted country, and PM2.5 particulates shortens the average Bangladeshi resident’s life by almost 7 years.
All 165 million Bangladeshi residents are exposed to an annual average PM 2.5 pollution level that exceeds both the WHO guideline and Bangladesh’s national standard of 15 µg/m3.
Since 1998, PM2.5 pollution has increased by 63 percent.
Particulate pollution is the second greatest threat to human health in Bangladesh, behind cardiovascular diseases.

title: Air Quality
text:
How Is It Made?
Annual measures of air quality are done by MODIS, MISR, and SeaWiFS. The air quality is measured annually using satellite-retrieved aerosol optical depth (AOD) and accounts for daily air quality readings. Using geographically weighted regression to account for variations of data such as seasonality, the data is aggregated to a resolution of 1 km to give an annual measure of µg/m3 for the year 2019.

title: Air Quality
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
The map displays the 1 km grid cells in measurements of µg/m3. As the Bangladesh national standard is 15 µg/m3, areas that exceed this number may require various air quality interventions.

title: Vegetation
text:
What Is It?
This map shows the density and health of vegetation, as observed by satellite sensors. Specifically, it shows the normalized difference vegetation index, or NDVI, which uses visible and non-visible light to quantify vegetation on a scale of -1 to 1. Values below 0.1 indicate water, rock, or artificial surfaces, and higher values indicate greater vegetation density and health.

title: Vegetation
text:
Why Use It?
Knowing the distribution and density of plant life in and around a city is helpful for a variety of reasons. NDVI data can inform urban planning, environmental management, and public health initiatives. More plant matter in cities is associated with health benefits and the mitigation of environmental risks. Vegetation in a city can reduce temperatures and the urban heat island effect, and is an important component of passive cooling. It can also lessen air pollution by means of the deposition, dispersion and modification of pollutant particulate matter. Plants absorb rainwater and slow the flow of surface water to reduce flooding (though, flooding can also increase in areas where flow has slowed), and hold hillsides together to reduce erosion in the event of flooding, as well as increasing evaporation. Increased vegetation may even alter an area’s climate, which in turn affects the frequency and magnitude of flooding. Green spaces in a city can also serve important civic, social, and quality of life functions, by offering spaces for gathering and activity. This role can be especially crucial during health events, such as the COVID-19 pandemic, when the increased air flow of outdoor spaces made it often healthier to spend time outside rather than inside.
This map shows the median distribution of vegetation around a city, but NDVI is also useful when different time periods are compared. With multiple data points across time, NDVI can show such things as the increase and decrease of biomass, the health of plants, and the presence of drought. (The City Scan also uses NDMI, the normalized difference moisture index, for measuring drought.)

title: Vegetation
text:
How Is It Made?
NDVI is calculated using remote sensing data, either from satellite or aerial vehicles. In this particular project, the remote sensing data is collected by the Copernicus Programme’s (/European Space Agency’s) Sentinel-2 mission’s two satellites, which capture multiple ranges of light reflected from the earth’s surface. By combining these ranges in different ways, different phenomena on earth can be observed. Vegetation is highlighted by combining the near-infrared radiation and red radiation bands: NDVI takes the difference of near-infrared radiation (\(NIR\)) and red radiation (\(Red\)) and divides it by the two bands’ sum.
\[NDVI = \frac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}\]
It may seem odd that this measurement uses the red, rather than green, radiation band. Plants appear green to the human eye because, within the visible spectrum, they strongly absorb red and blue light, and reflect green light. However, plants are typically much more reflective of near infrared light, and the comparison of near infrared radiation to red radiation is more indicative of vegetation than the comparison of green radiation to red radiation.
The user generating the map has the option to plot year-round NDVI or the NDVI of the hottest months. The choice may depend on the specific geography: a user may choose to plot the seasonal NDVI values to diminish the effect of colder months when plants are dormant and emitting lower NDVI values, or when snow cover will yield negative NDVI values. Alternatively, a user may plot year-round values in a place with hot and dry summers, where vegetation is similarly dormant due to dryness. Both the year-round and seasonal values take the average monthly value from 2014 to 2023, with the seasonal values using the three on-average hottest months, as identified in the land surface temperature calculation.

title: Vegetation
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
NDVI ranges between -1 and 1, with higher numbers indicating a higher density of healthy vegetation. Values of less than 0.1 typically indicate water, rock, and otherwise barren land; values of 0.1 to 0.3 are associated with shrubs and grassland; 0.3 to 0.5 corresponds to moderate vegetation; and values of more than 0.5 correspond to dense vegetation such as forests or mature crops. These numbers, though, vary by place and ecology. Different plant types emit different NDVI patterns. Because of this, an area might show lower NDVI values even while boasting lots of vegetation. In addition, NDVI does not separate plant density and plant health, nor does it account for the spatial arrangement of plant matter within an areal unit.
Because this map shows an average NDVI (whether an average of all twelve months or the hottest three), it does not show seasonality. An area may feature high vegetation density and health during one season of the year, and low vegetation during the rest of the year. In the year-round average, these high vegetation seasons will be suppressed; in the 3-month average, the high vegetation may be overly prominent or fully excluded.

title: Vegetation
text:
Source Data Citation

European Space Agency. 2020. “Normalized Difference Vegetation Index”. Sentinel-2. https://documentation.dataspace.copernicus.eu/Data/SentinelMissions/Sentinel2.html

title: Summer Land Surface Temperature (LST)
text:
What Is It?
Summer land surface temperature (LST) refers to the temperature of the Earth’s surface as measured during the three hottest months. Unlike air temperature, which is typically measured at about 1.5 to 2 meters above the ground, LST represents the actual temperature of the land or ground surface itself, including surfaces like soil, vegetation, and urban infrastructure.

title: Summer Land Surface Temperature (LST)
text:
Why Use It?
Temperatures in an area are affected by many factors, such as land cover, elevation, slope, and proximity to water. Higher temperatures can generate or exacerbate negative effects related to health, social equity, and economic productivity. Typically, cities demonstrate higher temperatures than vegetated areas: construction materials, such as concrete, absorb more solar radiation; less vegetation results in less evapotranspiration; and more vehicle usage and mechanical cooling generate more heat.
Note that it measures surface temperature rather than ambient temperature, which can differ by several degrees. Surface temperature is primarily useful for identifying hotter and cooler areas within a specific geography.

title: Summer Land Surface Temperature (LST)
text:
How Is It Made?
Data on land surface temperature is taken from June through September in 2014 to 2023 and the average value was extracted for every grid cell at a resolution of 30 meters. The product is generated from Landsat collection 2 Level 1 thermal infrared bands.

title: Summer Land Surface Temperature (LST)
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
LST is a great way to measure the impacts of human thermal comfort and heat stress. While LST is a good indicator of surface heat, it does not necessarily indicate other factors like humidity, wind, or direct sunlight. Moreover, LST may often get mistaken for measuring urban heat island effect, but it does not since urban heat island measures nighttime heat retention. However, it can give a general overview of hotter and cooler areas within a specific geography.

title: Summer Land Surface Temperature (LST)
text:
Source Data Citation

title: Economic Change
text:
What Is It?
Economic change measures the monthly variation in nighttime light intensity across urban areas, using data from the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS). Whereas the economic activity layer, which shows cumulative radiance levels, this map displays the 10-year trend in radiance, offering a dynamic view of shifts in economic activity. Positive values represent an increase in nighttime lights emission and by proxy, economic activity, whereas negative values represent a decrease in nighttime lights emission and economic activity.

title: Economic Change
text:
Why Use It?
Change in economic activity introduces a temporal dimension. By tracking shifts in nighttime light intensity over time, this layer reveals spatial patterns of economic transformation across an urban region. It is particularly useful for detecting emerging trends such as the growth of new commercial corridors, the expansion of urban fringes, the gradual recovery of previously inactive zones, or the movement of commerce or industry away from a prior center.

title: Economic Change
text:
How Is It Made?
The monthly nighttime light data is processed and extracted in the same way as the economic activity data. Rather than aggregating the monthly averages, the economic change analysis fits a linear regression model to the most recent 120-months of data for each raster cell. The value displayed for each cell is the month coefficient.

title: Economic Change
text:
How Should We Interpret It?
Areas with positive values have demonstrated an increase in nighttime light emissions, on the whole, in the most recent 10 years, which may denote an increase in economic activity. Conversely, negative values may indicate a decrease in economic activity. However, since nighttime lights are only a proxy, not a direct measure of economic output, these signals should be interpreted with caution. The same caveats as those noted for the economic activity layer apply here.

title: Economic Change
text:
Source Data Citation
